title: Expense Ratio Calculator — True Cost of Investment Fees
shortDescription: See how much expense ratios actually cost you over decades. Compare a 1% fee vs a 0.05% index fund — the gap can exceed your initial investment.
description: Enter principal, monthly contribution, years, expected return, and the fund's expense ratio. The calculator runs three scenarios — no fees, low-cost index (0.05%), and your fee — showing how much money you lose to expenses over time. The dollar gap is usually shocking once compounded over decades.
metaDescription: Free expense ratio calculator. See the true cost of investment fees over 30 years. Compare 1% actively managed funds vs 0.05% index funds. Includes monthly contributions.
empty: All values must be non-negative; years must be positive.

input.expenseRatioHint: Index funds: 0.03-0.20%. Active mutual funds: 0.5-1.5%. Some 401(k) plans: 1-2%.

result.lowFeeSavings: Switching to a 0.05% index fund would save {amount} over this period.

chart.heading: Balance by year: with vs. without fees
chart.caption: Both lines compound the same gross return; the lower line only subtracts the annual expense ratio.

sections.heading: Why fees matter more than you think
paragraphs: A 1% expense ratio sounds tiny, but it's not 1% of your contribution — it's 1% of your entire balance every year. Compounded over 30 years, a 1% fee can erase 25-30% of your final balance. That's not the fee being '1% per year' — that's the fee compounding against your gains, year after year.

sections.heading: The index fund advantage
paragraphs: Vanguard's VTSAX charges 0.04%. iShares ITOT charges 0.03%. Most 401(k) target date funds charge 0.10-0.50%. Many actively managed funds charge 0.50-1.50%. Decades of data show that low-cost index funds beat 80%+ of actively managed funds over 15-year periods, primarily because the actively managed funds have to overcome their own fee drag.

sections.heading: What this calculator can't capture
paragraphs: Trading costs (bid-ask spreads, market impact) are excluded. Tax drag from active fund turnover (active funds typically realize 50-100% of holdings per year, generating capital gains tax in taxable accounts) can add another 0.5-1.5% effective drag. Advisor fees (1% of AUM) compound on top of the fund's expense ratio. Add all together and 'low-cost' becomes a 2-3% advantage over 'standard.'

sections.heading: Formula and sources
paragraphs: Both curves compound the same gross return monthly; the only difference is that the expense ratio is subtracted from the with-fee curve's growth rate ((return − fee) ÷ 12 per month). The widening gap between the two lines is compounding working against you.

The SEC's Investor Bulletin on fees uses the same before/after comparison and shows that even a 1% annual fee can consume a substantial share of a long-term portfolio (see Sources). To look up a real fund's expense ratio, use FINRA's Fund Analyzer linked below.

faq.a: Yes. On $500,000 invested for 30 years at 7% nominal return, a 1% expense ratio costs about $700,000 vs $130,000 for a 0.05% index fund. The fee compounds, the gains it kills compound, and the gains those would have produced compound. Most people hugely underestimate this.

Under 0.20% is excellent. 0.20-0.50% is acceptable for niche or international funds. 0.50-1.0% is hard to justify outside specialty strategies. Anything above 1% should require a strong reason — usually it's a 401(k) plan with no choice, in which case maximize match and complain to HR.

faq.q: Why not just pick the best-performing fund?
faq.a: Past performance is uncorrelated with future performance. Past expense ratios are perfectly correlated with future expense ratios (they don't change much). Picking on fees is the only signal that reliably predicts long-run outcomes.

Fidelity offers four ZERO funds (FZROX, FZILX, FNILX, FZIPX) with 0.00% expense ratios. Caveat: they're only available in Fidelity accounts and have non-standard indexes. Otherwise 0.03-0.04% is as low as it gets at Vanguard, Schwab, iShares.

faq.q: What about ETF vs mutual fund?
faq.a: For the same index, ETFs and mutual funds usually have identical or near-identical expense ratios. ETFs can have small advantages in taxable accounts due to in-kind redemptions reducing capital gain distributions. For tax-advantaged accounts (IRA, 401k), pick whatever's cheaper or simpler.

faq.q: How do I find my fund's expense ratio?
faq.a: Look up the fund's ticker on Morningstar, the fund company's site, or your brokerage. The prospectus also lists it under 'Fund Operating Expenses.' If you can't find it within 30 seconds, that's a yellow flag for fund transparency.

faq.q: Is this calculator pessimistic about active management?
faq.a: It models pure fee drag, not whether active managers add or subtract value. The empirical record shows active managers don't reliably beat their indexes net of fees — but a few do. The calculator just shows you what you're paying for that lottery ticket.

faq.q: Should I switch funds based on this?
faq.a: In a tax-advantaged account, yes — there's no tax cost to switching. In a taxable account, weigh the capital gains tax against the future fee savings. Often switching to a cheaper fund pays for the tax bill within 2-5 years; over a 30-year horizon it's nearly always worth it.

steps.name: Find each fund's expense ratio
steps.text: Look up each holding on Morningstar or the fund company's website.

steps.name: Calculate your weighted average
steps.text: Multiply each fund's expense ratio by its weight in your portfolio, sum them. Aim for under 0.20%.

steps.name: Run this calculator
steps.text: Enter your portfolio's weighted expense ratio. See the dollar cost over your time horizon.

steps.name: Identify the worst offenders
steps.text: Sort holdings by expense ratio. The top 1-2 are usually your worst leaks.

steps.name: Replace if cheaper alternatives exist
steps.text: For broad index exposure (S&P 500, total market, total international), there's almost always a 0.03-0.10% option. Replace anything above 0.30% unless it covers a market you can't get cheaper.
